schools.summary: Luke's precise dating (Tiberius's 15th year, Pilate, the tetrarchs) to fix the time exactly; the acceptable year of the Lord.

Gregory/Origen: the naming of the Gentile emperor and the Jewish rulers signals a gospel for Jew and Gentile; the divided kingdom marks the appointed hour; Bede details Rome's control of the priesthood.
Henry: the dating shows a conquered people — the sceptre departed from Judah (Gen 49:10), so Shiloh must come.
Two high priests (Annas deposed but influential, Caiaphas acting) betray a corrupt, Rome-managed priesthood; the word of God comes to John as to the prophets (Jer 1:2).
Chrysostom: the word was a commandment, John came not of himself; Ambrose: the Church begins not from man but the Word; Theophylact: John hidden in the desert lest his witness seem mere kinship.
Henry: prophecy long suspended revived in John; the word is not lost in a wilderness — God finds his servants anywhere.

themes: the word of God came to John

schools.summary: John comes from the desert to the Jordan region; the baptism of repentance — a familiar washing whose meaning was repentance, both binding to turn from sin and sealing pardon to the penitent (Ezek 18:30; Acts 13:24).
Ambrose: the wilderness whence he came figures the Church, the barren bearing more children than the wedded.
Henry: those happiest in retirement must come forth when God calls; the baptism both obliged to repentance and assured of pardon.
John fulfils Isaiah 40:3 (John 1:23); Luke quotes the prophecy more fully than Matthew/Mark; the 'way' and 'paths' are the hearts to be prepared for Christ.
Ambrose: John is the voice, the Word follows — his dignity lies wholly in the One he heralds, not in himself.
Luke alone extends the Isaiah citation (40:4); the levelling of the land read as the gospel's moral work — raising the lowly, abasing the proud, straightening the crooked, smoothing the rough.
Henry: the humble enriched, the proud humbled (to dust if penitent, to hell if not), the crooked heart made straight by grace, the way to heaven made plain.

The climax of Luke's citation (Isa 40:5): the salvation of God seen by all flesh — Jew and Gentile — fitting in the Gospel of the universal Saviour (Isa 52:10; Titus 2:11); 'salvation of God' = Christ himself.
Henry: the salvation more fully discovered and spread wider — when the way is made in the heart, bid the salvation of God welcome.

themes: the goal of the prepared way

schools.summary: John reproves the crowds fearlessly (Matthew names Pharisees/Sadducees, Matt 3:7); his metaphors are from the wilderness; 'wrath to come' = the final judgment hypocrites ignore while spared (Calvin).

Chrysostom: Scripture names men by beasts for their passions — vipers for poison and cunning, parricides like the Jews who killed their teachers, yet without blaming the Patriarchs; Cyril of Jerusalem: put off the serpent's slough, baptism a redemption from the fire.

Chrysostom/Gregory: repentance must bear fruit (the salve as well as the arrow drawn), worthy and proportioned to guilt; the stones = the Gentiles, faith poured into stony hearts to raise children to Abraham (Ambrose, Gregory of Nyssa).
Descent from Abraham avails nothing without the virtue of kinship (John 8:39; Rom 4:12); Lightfoot notes a possible wordplay on 'stones'/'children' deriding the national boast.
Wesley: trust not in church membership or privilege — God requires a change of heart without delay.

The axe 'laid near' (not fixed at) the root — a remnant yet saved (Cyril of Jerusalem); Gregory: the tree = the human race, the axe = the Redeemer striking by God's power at the root; the fruitless tree for the fire (Matt 7:19; Luke 13:7).
Gill: the Romans are the axe in God's hand, about to bring ruin on the nation, city, and temple.

The preaching pierces the conscience (Acts 2:37; 16:30); three classes inquire (multitude, publicans, soldiers); the questions peculiar to Luke, the physician of the soul, showing guidance for the conscience after confession (Ellicott).
Calvin: true repentance desires to know God's will; John points to the duties of charity (second Table) as the surer mark, less liable to hypocrisy than ceremonies.
Gregory/Origen: the troubled hearers seek advice; three classes inquire in turn.

To the crowds, practical mercy — sharing even necessaries (Gregory), out of love of neighbour (Basil), yet in proportion, not depriving oneself of all (Ambrose); John practised what he taught (Cyril of Jerusalem).
Religion's first demand is to do good, requiring self-denial and imitating Christ who became poor (2 Cor 8:14; 1 John 3:17; James 2:15); the coat is the inner undergarment.
Calvin: deeds of charity testify true piety, detecting the hypocrisy of those who profess with the mouth what is far from the heart.
The despised, oppressive tax-collectors (telos + ōneomai; Jews collecting for Rome by graft) come penitent; their presence implied in Matt 21:32; they ask the same 'what shall we do?'
Bede/Chrysostom: the power of John's word that even publicans seek salvation — the rich seeking the way from the poor.
Calvin: repentance demands the duties peculiar to each calling, not general sorrow only.

John bids the publicans not quit but reform — 'exact no more than appointed'; the Roman farming system bred extortion (Ellicott); the office itself honourable and necessary, government must be supported (Rom 13:6).
Bede: keep from desiring others' goods, that they may come to share their own.

themes: a lawful office purged of extortion

schools.summary: Likely Herod's troops (perhaps marching against Aretas); 'do violence' (diaseisēte, extortion by intimidation, Latin concutere); 'accuse falsely' (sykophantēsēte, the informer); 'wages' (opsōnia, rations/pay; 1 Cor 9:7; Rom 6:23) — discontent a mercenary's vice.
Augustine: soldiers using arms are ministers of the law, not homicides; war's evils are cruelty and lust of power, not the office — so John reforms rather than forbids it.
Calvin: John bids them not throw away arms but cease to pillage — the calling lawful, its abuses condemned.

debates.question: Is military service lawful for the penitent?

sides.position: Yes — the office is lawful, only its abuses (extortion, false accusation, cruelty) are condemned

schools.summary: The people's deference threatens to overreach (Origen: uncontrolled love is dangerous); Ambrose: their expectation absurd; John saw their hearts by God's gift, distinguishing the baptism of repentance from that of grace.
The people muse whether John is the Christ; the priests and Levites sent to ask (John 1:19-20); John gloried not but embraced humility.
John publicly disclaims Messiahship; 'mightier than I' (no comparison possible — Ambrose); 'baptize with Holy Ghost and fire' both the spiritual baptism and the judgment-fire (Acts 1:5; Matt 3:11).

Augustine reconciles 'bear'/'unloose' the shoes as one sense of humility; Gregory: the shoes figure the Incarnation's mystery John cannot fathom (the flesh as our corruption); Chrysostom: Christ's baptism excels John's water.

The fan/winnowing as the Messiah's sifting judgment (Jer 15:7; Ps 1:4); wheat gathered to the garner (Micah 4:12; Matt 13:30), chaff burned with unquenchable fire (Mark 9:48) — everlasting punishment for the impenitent.
Bede: the fan separates wheat from chaff as the wind does, testing to reveal hidden virtue or expose unbelief.

themes: wheat to the garner, chaff to fire

schools.summary: Luke gives only a sample of John's many messages (cf. John 21:25); the verb is 'preach good news' — his exhortation was gospel, pointing to the Messiah and to faith in him; Luke alone stresses the wider range.
Origen/Theophylact: John, like Christ, said many things too great to record; his 'exhortation' is fitly called Gospel, the telling of good things.

Herod the tetrarch (distinguished from the nativity Herod) reproved (elenchō, convict/expose) for marrying Herodias, his brother Philip's wife (Matt 14:3; Lev 18:16), and for all his evils; Josephus confirms the marriage's repulsiveness.
Calvin: John an example of the moral courage every minister needs — to reprove the great without acceptance of persons, whatever the cost.

The imprisonment crowns Herod's crimes ('above all' = one more like the rest, not necessarily the worst — Barnes); Luke records it out of order to conclude John's history before turning to Christ (Wesley, Bede, Gloss).
Calvin: 'above all' marks malice grown desperate — enraged by the remedy, taking vengeance on the reprover.

Christ baptized not to be cleansed but to cleanse the waters and fulfil all righteousness (Matt 3:15), burying the old Adam; the opened heaven shows baptism's virtue — the kingdom's gates flung open (Bede).
Chrysostom distinguishes the Jewish, John's, and Christian baptisms; Christ needed neither pardon nor the Spirit; Luke's 'praying' teaches constant prayer after baptism.

themes: Jesus baptized to cleanse, not be cleansed

schools.summary: The Spirit in 'bodily shape' (peculiar to Luke) like a dove, seen by the Baptist (John 1:33); the Trinity manifest, the deity and Sonship of Christ plain; the dove an emblem of purity.

Ambrose: the dove signifies innocence and peace (the ark's olive branch); Chrysostom: meekness (vs Pentecost's fire), the Spirit received for us as Founder of our race; Athanasius: 'beloved Son' by nature, not grace — the Only-begotten, against Arius.

Luke's genealogy ascends from Christ; 'about thirty' the age of full maturity for ministry; 'as was supposed son of Joseph' — really of Mary, both of David's house (Luke 1:27); Luke traces through Nathan (2 Sam 5:14), Matthew through Solomon.

Origen: the line carried up to God; Gregory Nazianzen/Chrysostom: thirty years teach humility and fulfilling the whole law; Eusebius: Matthew gives the truth and descends into the flesh, Luke gives the common opinion and ascends to the regeneration.

debates.question: How are Matthew's and Luke's genealogies reconciled?

sides.position: Levirate succession — Joseph son of both Jacob (Matthew) and Heli (Luke)
Matthew gives the true legal line; Luke records the 'supposed' common reckoning, ascending and omitting the sinful kings
Luke traces Mary's line (through Nathan), Matthew Joseph's (through Solomon)

themes: as was supposed son of Joseph

schools.summary: Obscure post-exilic links (Matthat, Levi, Melchi, Janna, Joseph) unknown elsewhere; a textual question whether Matthat and Levi belong (Grotius vs the versions); part of Luke's ascent to God (Matt 1:1).

themes: no link in God's purpose lost

schools.summary: Unrecorded post-exilic generations (Mattathias, Amos, Naum, Esli, Nagge); little remembered of them; Luke's humbler ascending line preserving David's seed through the silent years until the fulness of time (Gal 4:4).

The obscure descent continues (Maath, Mattathias, Semei, Joseph, Juda), names unknown elsewhere; Scripture traces the legal line of the husband (Ambrose); God's purpose advancing through ordinary faithfulness toward Christ.

The line touches Zerubbabel of the return (Ezra 5:2; Haggai 1:1); Lightfoot on the name-changes in Babylon (Zerubbabel = 'winnowing of Babel') and the convergence at Salathiel/Neri vs Matthew's Jechonias (Matt 1:12; 1 Chron 3:19).

Gill: these pre-exilic links were of David's house in the line of Nathan — Luke's distinctive descent through Nathan (not Solomon's royal kings), a path apart from the curse on the royal line (cf. v.23 discussion).
Unremembered links in the Nathan branch (Simeon, Juda, Joseph, Jonan, Eliakim); their very anonymity attesting Christ's true, traceable human descent (Heb 2:17) within Luke's ascent to God.

themes: the human roots of the Saviour

schools.summary: The line reaches David through Nathan, his son born in Jerusalem (2 Sam 5:14; Zech 12:12), not the prophet — the node where Luke and Matthew diverge; David the recipient of the messianic promise (Luke 1:32).

themes: Son of David, heir of the throne

schools.summary: Gill: from Jesse back the line agrees exactly with Matthew and the OT (Ruth 4:18-22; Matt 1:5) — Jesse, Obed, Boaz, Salmon, Nahshon, the close of Ruth; the Gentile strand (Ruth, Rahab) foreshadowing salvation for all.

themes: agreement with Matthew and the OT

schools.summary: The line reaches the patriarchs — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, back through Terah and Nahor (Gen 11:24-26); where Matthew began, Luke passes through toward Adam; Abraham the covenant centre, Christ the promised Seed (Gen 12:3; Gal 3:16).

The post-flood patriarchs of Shem's line (Gen 11:16-18): Serug, Reu, Peleg, Eber, Salah; Eber the father of the Hebrew stock; Peleg, in whose days the earth was divided (Gen 10:25) — Christ kinsman of all nations descended from Noah.

The famous crux of the second Cainan (son of Arphaxad), absent from the Hebrew, Samaritan, Targum, and Josephus but present in the LXX Luke follows (Gen 11:12); Gill judges it a transcriber's intrusion, Clarke a possible surname; the line runs sure through Shem and Noah (Gen 10:21).

debates.question: Is the second 'Cainan' (son of Arphaxad) original to Luke?

sides.position: A later transcriber's intrusion, not original; absent from the Hebrew OT
Luke followed the Septuagint, which has it; perhaps a surname of Salah

themes: the line through Noah and Shem

schools.summary: The antediluvian patriarchs (Gen 5): Methuselah the longest-lived, Enoch who walked with God and was translated (Gen 5:24) and prophesied the Lord's coming (Jude 14), Jared, Mahalaleel, the first Cainan; the line reaches back to the dawn of mankind.

themes: translated that he should not die
the line to the dawn of history

schools.summary: The line climbs through Enos and Seth to Adam, 'the son of God' by creation (Acts 17:28; Gen 1:26; 2:7); Luke ascends to God (Origen); Christ shown son of Adam and of God, kinsman of all mankind — the two Adams (1 Cor 15:45; Luke 1:35).

themes: the second Adam joined to the first
